data.description:

Approach 1: Cigna Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of the cash a company could generate in the future and discounts those amounts back to what they might be worth in today’s dollars. It is a way of tying the share price to the business’s own cash generation rather than only to market multiples.

For Cigna Group, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $7.0b. Analysts supply detailed forecasts for the earlier years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates further out, including a projected Free Cash Flow of $9.5b in 2030. These projections are discounted back to today using a required return, producing an estimated intrinsic value per share of about $815.34.

Compared with the recent share price of US$278.95, the DCF output implies the stock is about 65.8% undervalued according to this specific model and its assumptions.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Cigna Group.

Approach 2: Cigna Group Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Cigna Group, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings the business is currently generating. It helps you see how many dollars investors are paying today for one dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth outlook and risk profile. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher perceived risk tend to line up with a lower P/E. Cigna Group currently trades on a P/E of 12.12x, compared with the Healthcare industry average of about 22.93x and a peer group average of 31.25x.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what Cigna Group’s P/E might be, given its earnings growth characteristics, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk profile. For Cigna Group, this Fair Ratio is 28.53x. Because it is tailored to the company’s own fundamentals, it can be more informative than a simple comparison with broad industry or peer averages. With the current P/E of 12.12x sitting well below the Fair Ratio of 28.53x, this approach suggests that the shares may be trading below their estimated value on an earnings basis.

Result: UNDERVALUED

However, the P/E might be low for a reason and it requires further investigation to determine if it's justified.

Is There Any Growth For Angi?

The only time you'd be truly comfortable seeing a P/E as low as Angi's is when the company's growth is on track to lag the market.

Retrospectively, the last year delivered a frustrating 6.6% decrease to the company's bottom line. At least EPS has managed not to go completely backwards from three years ago in aggregate, thanks to the earlier period of growth. Therefore, it's fair to say that earnings growth has been inconsistent recently for the company.

Turning to the outlook, the next three years should generate growth of 20% per annum as estimated by the eight analysts watching the company. That's shaping up to be materially higher than the 12% per annum growth forecast for the broader market.

With this information, we find it odd that Angi is trading at a P/E lower than the market. It looks like most investors are not convinced at all that the company can achieve future growth expectations.

The Final Word

Generally, our preference is to limit the use of the price-to-earnings ratio to establishing what the market thinks about the overall health of a company.

We've established that Angi currently trades on a much lower than expected P/E since its forecast growth is higher than the wider market. When we see a strong earnings outlook with faster-than-market growth, we assume potential risks are what might be placing significant pressure on the P/E ratio. data.title: Aritzia (TSX:ATZ) Same Store Sales Surge Tests Bulls’ Expansion Narrative
data.description:
Aritzia (TSX:ATZ) has put up another busy quarter, with Q3 2026 revenue of about C$1.0b and basic EPS of C$1.20, alongside net income of C$138.9 million and same store sales growth of 34.3%. The company has seen revenue move from C$728.7 million and EPS of C$0.66 in Q3 2025 to C$1.0b and C$1.20 in Q3 2026, with trailing twelve month revenue at C$3.4b and EPS of C$3.03 setting the backdrop for how investors read these numbers. With trailing net profit margins now higher than a year ago, the latest results keep the focus squarely on how sustainable this profitability profile looks.

Same Store Sales Surge to 34.3%

  • Same store sales growth moved from 6.6% in Q3 2025 to 34.3% in Q3 2026, alongside quarterly revenue of C$1.0b and net income of C$138.9 million.
  • Analysts' consensus view ties a lot of Aritzia’s growth story to store and format expansion, and these numbers provide a way to sanity check that view:
    • The consensus narrative points to U.S. boutique openings and new flagships as key drivers of higher sales, and same store growth of 34.3% sits alongside Q3 revenue of C$1,040.3 million compared with C$728.7 million in Q3 2025.
    • That same narrative highlights stronger product assortment and lower markdowns as supporting margins, and trailing net profit of C$347.2 million on C$3.4b of revenue aligns with a focus on productivity per store rather than just adding more locations.
To see how this sales momentum relates to the broader growth story and market debate around Aritzia, analysts' consensus narrative goes into more depth about store expansion, digital plans, and margin expectations. 📊 Read the full Aritzia Consensus Narrative.

Margins Track Higher On Trailing Basis

  • On the trailing twelve month view, net profit margin is 10.2% on C$3.4b of revenue and C$347.2 million of net income, compared with 5.2% on C$2.5b of revenue and C$132.4 million a year earlier.
  • For the bullish narrative, a key point is how these profitability figures link to growth plans that focus on better mix and marketing rather than just volume:
    • The bullish side of the consensus narrative highlights lower markdowns and optimized inventory as supports for earnings, and the move from C$132.4 million in trailing net income at Q3 2025 to C$347.2 million at Q3 2026 is presented as consistent with that emphasis on margin quality.
    • Those same bulls point to digital and marketing investments as sales drivers, and trailing revenue rising from C$2.5b to C$3.4b gives them concrete volume figures to reference when arguing that past spending has translated into higher profitability.

P/E Premium Versus Growth Profile

  • Aritzia trades on a trailing P/E of 43.7x against an industry average of 21x and a peer average of 21.9x, with a stated DCF fair value of C$72.16 compared to a current share price of C$131.47.
  • Skeptics focus on that valuation gap, and the numbers here illustrate why they question how much of the growth is already priced in:
    • Bears point out that earnings are already described as high quality and have grown strongly on a trailing basis, yet the DCF fair value of C$72.16 is well below C$131.47, which they view as a sign that the market is paying a high multiple for those improvements.
    • They also note that analysts see earnings growing about 22.7% per year while the stock already trades at 43.7x, a level that is roughly double the 21x industry average, so any slower progress relative to those expectations could matter more at this price.
